name: Badajoz and Campo Maior Loop
name_original: Al Castillo de Campomaior por la ciudad de Badajoz — circular desde Alburquerque
description: Cycle 64.2 miles (103.4 km) from Alburquerque to the historic Castle of Campo Maior via Badajoz, a moderate road cycling adventure.
region_intro: Road cycling around Alburquerque features a diverse landscape, characterized by rolling hills and varied terrain suitable for different skill levels. The region offers routes that traverse open countryside, providing a mix of ascents and descents. Cyclists can expect to encounter moderate elevation changes across the area's road network. The environment supports both shorter, less demanding rides and longer, more challenging excursions. Best road cycling routes around Alburquerque The most popular road cycling route is Badajoz and Campo Maior Loop, a 64.2 miles (103.4 km) trail that takes 4 hours 45 minutes to complete. This moderate route features significant elevation gain, offering a comprehensive cycling experience. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Castillo de La Beltraneja – Castle of Luna loop from Alto de las Encinas, a moderate 40.7 miles (65.4 km) path. This route includes notable climbs and scenic views, making it a rewarding ride. Local road cyclists also love the Cabroeira Climb – São Mamede Trail loop from Marco, a 45.4 miles (73.1 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 52 minutes. Road cycling around Alburquerque is defined by its rolling hills, open landscapes, and challenging ascents. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more difficult climbs. The routes in Alburquerque are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 240 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Alburquerque's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Alburquerque?
A: Road cycling around Alburquerque is characterized by its rolling hills and varied terrain. You'll find a mix of ascents and descents across open countryside, offering moderate elevation changes suitable for various skill levels. The region supports both shorter, less demanding rides and longer, more challenging excursions.

Q: Are there routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy ride?
A: Yes, Alburquerque offers routes for different ability levels. While many routes feature moderate to difficult climbs, there is at least one easy route available. For a comprehensive overview, you can explore the various options on komoot.

Q: What are some of the more challenging road cycling routes in the Alburquerque area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31847731" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabroeira Climb – São Mamede Trail loop from Marco&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40015055" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabroeira Climb – São Mamede Trail loop from Piscinas Naturales de La Codosera&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gains and are rated as difficult. These routes provide rewarding climbs and scenic views.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Alburquerque?
A: The road cycling routes in Alburquerque are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 240 road cyclists have used komoot to explore the varied terrain, often praising the challenging ascents and open landscapes.

Q: Are there any notable landmarks or attractions to see along the cycling routes?
A: Yes, several routes pass by interesting historical and natural sites. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38163939" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de La Beltraneja – Castle of Luna loop from Alto de las Encinas&lt;/a&gt; takes you near the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1574176" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Luna&lt;/a&gt;. Other attractions in the region include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4429272" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Capela dos Ossos de Campo Maior&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4429266" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Campo Maior&lt;/a&gt;, and the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6545309" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabroeira Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find loop routes for road cycling in Alburquerque?
A: Many of the popular road cycling routes in Alburquerque are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993539796/badajoz-and-campo-maior-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Badajoz and Campo Maior Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40014935" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portalegre Castle – Colorful Portalegre loop from Piscinas Naturales de La Codosera&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: How long do the road cycling routes typically take to complete?
A: Route durations vary depending on distance and difficulty. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993539796/badajoz-and-campo-maior-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Badajoz and Campo Maior Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is over 100 km, typically takes around 4 hours and 45 minutes. Shorter routes, like the 65 km &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38163939" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de La Beltraneja – Castle of Luna loop from Alto de las Encinas&lt;/a&gt;, can be completed in about 3 hours.

Q: What is the longest road cycling route available in Alburquerque?
A: Among the featured routes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993539796/badajoz-and-campo-maior-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Badajoz and Campo Maior Loop&lt;/a&gt; is one of the longest, covering approximately 103.4 kilometers (64.2 miles).

Q: Are there any routes that feature castles?
A: Yes, several routes offer views of or pass by historical castles. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38163939" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de La Beltraneja – Castle of Luna loop from Alto de las Encinas&lt;/a&gt; is a great option, providing access to the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1574176" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Luna&lt;/a&gt;. You can also find routes near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4429266" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Campo Maior&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5549538" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ouguela Castle&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: How many road cycling routes are there in Alburquerque?
A: There are 18 road cycling routes available in the Alburquerque area, catering to various skill levels from easy to difficult.

Q: Is there a route that includes a waterfall?
A: While not directly on a featured road cycling route, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6545309" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabroeira Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; is a notable natural attraction in the Alburquerque region that could be visited as part of a cycling excursion or nearby a route like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31847731" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabroeira Climb – São Mamede Trail loop from Marco&lt;/a&gt;.
tips: Reservoir that is located in the west of the province of Badajoz and that collects mainly the waters of the Sierra de San Pedro that is located in its north. It is located close to the center of Villar del Rey, which gives it its name.
Old bridge that is over the Río Gevora and from where it is not possible to drive. A cycle path appears next to it, and where it is made of iron, you can ride on it safely.
Spectacular walled complex surrounding the town. You can go from several roads, both from Badajoz and Elvas.

faqs.question: Where does this cycling route start, and is there parking available in Alburquerque?
faqs.answer: This circular road cycling route begins and ends in Alburquerque. While the exact starting point can be flexible within the town, you'll find parking options in and around the town center, which is a convenient place to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty and terrain like for this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is considered to be of moderate difficulty, making it suitable for road cyclists with a reasonable level of fitness. The terrain is characterized by mostly paved surfaces. While the region of Extremadura has varied terrain, this specific route is designed to be manageable, though you should expect some elevation changes as you traverse the landscape.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest I will see along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable features on this route. Key highlights include the historical fortifications of Badajoz, such as the Alcazaba, and the impressive Castle of Campo Maior in Portugal. You'll also pass by the View of Villar del Rey Reservoir and cross the Bridge over the Gévora River, offering diverse scenery from historical architecture to natural vistas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route, crossing the border between Spain and Portugal, is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Does this route pass through any protected areas, and are there any permits or fees required?
faqs.answer: Yes, a portion of this route is located within ZPE São Vicente. Generally, cycling on established roads within such zones does not require special permits or entrance fees for individuals, but it's always advisable to respect local regulations regarding protected natural spaces. Ensure you stay on designated paths and do not disturb wildlife or vegetation.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty and length of over 100 kilometers, this route is generally more suited for experienced road cyclists or those with a good level of fitness. While it features mostly paved surfaces, the distance and elevation changes might be challenging for beginners or young children. There are likely easier, shorter routes available near Alburquerque if you're looking for a more family-friendly option.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through the city of Badajoz and the town of Campo Maior, you will find numerous opportunities for refreshments. Both locations offer cafes, restaurants, and shops where you can refuel and take a break. It's always a good idea to carry some snacks and water, especially for the stretches between towns.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: As this is primarily a road cycling route that traverses public roads and urban areas, dogs are generally permitted if kept on a leash and under control, especially in towns and near traffic. However, specific rules may apply within certain historical sites or natural parks like ZPE São Vicente, so always be mindful of local signage and regulations.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other official cycling or walking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route partially overlaps with several official trails. You will find yourself on sections of the Camino Natural del Corredor Cáceres - Badajoz, the Camino Natural Corredor Cáceres-Badajoz. Ruta Cáceres-Badajoz-Embalse de Piedra Aguda, and the Cañada Real de Sancha Brava. Smaller portions also coincide with the Camino Natural del Guadiana and the Defesas de Campo Maior.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect to see on this ride?
faqs.answer: The route offers a diverse range of scenery. You'll experience the historical urban landscapes of Badajoz with its ancient fortifications, the rural countryside of Extremadura and Alentejo, and the impressive historical architecture of the Castle of Campo Maior. Expect a mix of open fields, some forested areas, and views of reservoirs and rivers.

faqs.question: What should I pack or prepare for this cross-border cycling trip?
faqs.answer: For this route, ensure you have your passport or ID, as you'll be crossing the border into Portugal. Essential cycling gear includes a helmet, appropriate clothing for the weather, repair kit, pump, and plenty of water and snacks. Given the moderate difficulty and length, consider bringing a GPS device or a fully charged phone with the route downloaded. Sun protection is also highly recommended.

waypoints.name: View of Villar del Rey Reservoir
